docs.title: About
docs.text:
This work is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License.

These are the documentation pages for the sUAS rebuild of Arizona Game and Fish Department (AZGFD) heavy lift sUAS "Nakoda" by Institute for Computation & Data-enabled Insight (ICDI) and Data Science Institute (DSI) staff at The University of Arizona.

docs.title: Background
docs.text:
In 2019 AZGFD commissioned Arizona State University to develop a heavy lift sUAS (<20kg) that could deliver medicated food pellets to Gunnison's Prairie dogs in and around Black-footed Ferret Recovery.

The sUAS crashed several times, both during development and its early operational flights. After its last crash it was later shelved and has been in storage for ~5 years.

docs.title: Equipment
docs.text:
In January 2025 the Nakoda sUAS, its "mini-me" test sUAS, and all of its support equipment were delivered to me at University of Arizona.

AZGFD dropped off the drone with all of its associated support equipment and storage boxes. See Equipment for the list of items.

docs.title: Repair
docs.text:
In spring of 2025 we began to dissasemble the Nakoda UAS to inspect it and to clean its associated parts.

The first goal is to determine whether Nakoda (1) should be broken down for salvage, or (2) can be repaired for minimal cost and flown again.

See Repair Pages for details on the repair.

docs.title: Documentation
docs.text:
Nakoda did not arrive with any set of instructions for how to operate it, how to use or program the remote controls, how to charge or maintain the batteries, or how to use a Ground Control Station (GCS) with autonomous mission planning software.

I am creating and curating these documentation pages for posterity, and for AZGFD staff who will operate and maintain it.

docs.title: Batteries
docs.text:
This work is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License.

Drones use Lithium Polymer (LiPo) batteries for their high energy density to weight ratio.

Lithium polymer battery technology has greatly advanced in the last 5-years, and prices have come down considerably.

docs.title: Nakoda Batteries
docs.text:
Nakoda has 4 Tattu Plus 16,000 mAh batteries.

Each battery weighs ~2kg (!)

Charging each battery using a standard charger takes XXX minutes

| Voltage | Capacity | Amps |

docs.title: Mini-me Batteries
docs.text:
Mini-me has three Floureon 5500 mAh batteries.

The batteries are now ~5 years old, and will likely need to be replaced before re-entering service.

The Floureon batteries appear to be out of production. There are numerous other battery options.

docs.title: Battery Health
docs.text:
All of the Nakoda batteries arrived with zero charge on them. This is bad.

Tattu Manuals suggests charging and discharing the batteries very 3-months.

The Nakoda batteries are now ~5 years old, and will likely need to be replaced before re-entering service.

docs.title: Charging
docs.text:
AZGFD dropped off a EV Peak A9 battery charger and a MaxAmps 24 Volt DC Power Supply (two phase)

Tattu Battery Charging Details

docs.title: Battery Storage
docs.text: LiPo batteries are fire hazards. They must be stored in fire resistant containers, and be monitored for their condition and charge to ensure they do not

docs.title: Flight
docs.text: This work is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License.

docs.title: Pre-flight SOPs
docs.text:
Before leaving the station, it is a good idea to inventory all of the critical components to the mission plan.

These include, but are not limited to the following items

docs.title: Batteries Check
docs.text:
Ensure that all flight batteries are charged.

Ensure that Remote Controller battery is charged.

Ensure that back-up power charger is packed.

Ensure that generator is gassed up, or Electric battery bank is charged.

docs.title: Prop installation
docs.text:
Inspect all props for damage. Never fly with a cracked or deeply chipped propeller blade.

Ensure that additional props are packed.

If props are installed before leaving, ensure they are all installed appropriately for their left-handed (counter-clockwise) and right-handed (clockwise) positions.

docs.title: Taranis RC Control Bag
docs.text:
Ensure that Remote Control is packed

Ensure that Lanyard is packed.

Ensure that 110v charger cable is packed.

docs.title: AUTO Mode
docs.text: Uses a Ground Control Station

docs.text:
This work is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License.

Scheduled maintenance of the hardware should be made to ensure longevity of the equipment and safety of the operator.

docs.title: Description
docs.text:
The smaller copter that was delivered with the Nakoda drone has the same octocopter configuration.

Originally called "Bride of Chucky", we're calling it "mini-me"

docs.title: Motors
docs.text: Eight DJI 2212/920KV motors

docs.title: Electronic Speed Controller (ESC)
docs.text: 8 x Readytosky SimonK 30A ESC

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Quad-copter with eight motors

docs.title: Pixhawk4
docs.text:
The drone operates with a Pixhawk4, the firmware is TBD, but is likely the Ardupilot Copter quad with eight motors.

The Pixhawk4 PX4 Autopilot is an open industry standard autopilot.

docs.title: Wiring Chart
docs.text: Pixhawk4 Wiring Chart Overview

docs.title: GPS
docs.text:
Primary GPS is Single Pixhawk4 GPS on the main body, this GPS is also responsible for Integrated Compass, LED, Buzzer, and Safety Switch.

Currently, no RealTime Kinetics (RTK) GPS -- although there are options avaialble from Holybro

docs.title: FPV Camera
docs.text:
The drone had a FPV camera which could be used with a heads-up display or with FatShark FPV goggles.

We are planning to remove the FPV camera during the repair and will determine whether it offers any kind of core functionality critical to Nakoda's operation.

Currently, we believe that the drone does not need a FPV camera to operate (within line-of-sight of a S107 licensed operator, autopilot using a GCS MissionPlanner or QGround Control)

docs.title: Motors
docs.text: Eight U10ii KV100 efficiency multirotors.

docs.title: Electronic Speed Controllers (ESCs)
docs.text: 8 x ALPHA 60A 12S V1.2 FOC ESC

docs.title: Mission Planners
docs.text: This work is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License.

docs.title: What are Ground Control Stations (GCS)?
docs.text:
The Nakoda and Mini-me drone both operated on Ardupilot's Mission Planner software as its Ground Control Station (GCS) mission planning software.

GCS are necessary for monitoring on-board information, like remaining battery %, mission plan (survey, waypoints),

We are testing both Mission Planner and QGroundControl as options for the GCS.

Ground Control Stations (GCS)

A ground station is typically run from a ground-based computer, tablet, or phone. It transmits wireless telemetry to teh UAS via an USB antenna.

  • Mission Planner - Windows friendly, may not be fully Linux compatable.

  • QGroundControl - works in Linux, clean, modern look.

docs.title: Install Mission Planner
docs.text:
Mission Planner is ideally run on a Windows Laptop, but it can also be installed on Android and Linux.

Mission Planner Installation Instructions

docs.title: Installing QGroundControl
docs.text: Installation Instructions

docs.title: Ubuntu installation
docs.text:
sudo usermod -a -G dialout $USER
sudo apt-get remove modemmanager -y
sudo apt install gstreamer1.0-plugins-bad gstreamer1.0-libav gstreamer1.0-gl -y
sudo apt install libfuse2 -y
sudo apt install libxcb-xinerama0 libxkbcommon-x11-0 libxcb-cursor-dev -y

Install (and run) using the terminal commands:

mkdir QGroundControl
cd QGroundControl
wget https://d176tv9ibo4jno.cloudfront.net/builds/master/QGroundControl-x86_64.AppImage

Download QGroundControl.AppImage

chmod +x ./QGroundControl.AppImage
./QGroundControl.AppImage  (or double click)

docs.title: Remote ID
docs.text: This work is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License.

docs.title: What is Remote ID?
docs.text:
Remote ID is the FAA's required signal broadcasting for all drones weighing more than 255 grams.

Remote ID is required as of March 2024.

All drones, including DIY drones, must use Remote ID while in operation.

https://pilotinstitute.com/homebuilt-remote-id-rules/

docs.title: Getting an ID
docs.text: https://www.faa.gov/uas/getting_started/remote_id

docs.title: Setting up the Remote ID radio on Pixhawk4
docs.text: https://docs.px4.io/main/en/peripherals/remote_id.html

docs.title: Repair
docs.text: This work is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License.

docs.title: Cleaning
docs.text:
Dust is the enemy of electric motors and electronics.

All storage bins were washed and dried, spare parts were wiped with a dry microfiber cloth.

Electronics and motors were blasted with compressed air and wiped with Q-tips or microfiber cloth.

Strongly recommend dust abatement for keeping motors and connections dust free or clean routinely after flights.

docs.title: Batteries
docs.text:
The 16,000 mAh Tattu batteries were allowed to completely discharge over the last few years (they were likely manufactured and purchased in 2019 and last used in 2020). This is not good. LiPo batteries require being put into a "storage" charge mode and cycled every 3-6 months. The prolonged storage of the batteries may have resulted in degredation of the cells.

Because the Tattu batteries were completely discharged, I used the battery charger to 'trickle' charge a couple hundred mAh back into the batteries. I then used the 'balance' option to level the cells.

While doing this, X of the batteries failed to take any charge into their cells.

I was able to recover X batteries and charged them to 3.7v - 3.8v for 'storage' mode.

Two of the three Floureon 5500 mAh batteries exhibited 'swelling'.

Swelling occurs in LiPo batteries when they are left in a fully charged or overcharged state for a long period of time, with age, or prolonged harsh environmental conditions.

Swollen batteries should not be used in flight, as they are a fire hazard. Strongly recommend disposing of these batteries and buying new ones.

docs.title: Carbon Body
docs.text:
The Carbon body of Nakoda is intact, but I am leary about its design and load bearing strength.

Further, the battery is currently housed above the Pixhawk and PDB. This higher center of gravity may be affecting the flight behavior of the octocopter.

docs.title: Carbon Arms
docs.text: Nakoda is designed to lift heavy weight at its centroid, while all of its thrust is coming from the ends of its arms. This tension results in the arms bending upward and the Hopper swinging like a pendulum beneath it.

docs.title: Metal brackets
docs.text: There are metal brackets which hold the landing gear and antenna

docs.title: Wires and soldering points
docs.text: The soldering of the PDB on Mini-me was concerning, some of the hot and ground wires were nearly soldered together (or were in fact touching). There was melted plastic as well, suggesting arcing had occured.

docs.title: Power Distribution Board
docs.text:
I inspected the PDB on the Mini-me drone and found that it had signs of arcing (melted plastic, burns)

The PDB was replaced on Mini-me with the unopened PX4 PDB board. I soldered removed the cables from the old PDB and soldered them to the new board. I tested the current for all of the connections before connecting the 5500 mAh battery.

docs.title: ESCs
docs.text:
When I connected the motors and ESCs to the new PDB board, one of the ESCs (Motor 7) immediately began to smoke. I disconnected and removed the ESC and replaced it.

Wiring for the ESCs was incomplete.

docs.title: Motors
docs.text:
The motors were inspected.

The wiring for the Motors to the PDB for Ardupilot is:

docs.title: Propellers
docs.text:
I inspected all of the Nakoda 30" T-Motor Carbon Fiber (CF) 30 inch 10.5 degree propellers.

There are both left handed (counter-clockwise) and right handed (clockwise) propellers.

Several of the propellers are damaged. Most have minor damage, which may cause vibration and drag. Several have major damage, and one is broken. I used a paint marker to indicate which props are failing or should not be used.

Important: these props appear to be out of production, and are of limited availability from online retailers, costs are $280 to $335 per pair.

docs.title: Telemetry Radio
docs.text:
There was no telemetry radio delivered by AZGFD.

The inventory list has a USB Antenna for attaching to a field laptop for the Ground Control Station, but no transmitter for the drone.

I have ordered a new Telemetry radio.

docs.title: Remote ID
docs.text:
Remote ID is required as of March 2024.

I have ordered a Remote ID transmitter.
